walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Viticulture set up! Place the main game board in the center of the table. This board shows the seasonal action spaces where you will place your workers throughout the year. The board is divided into four seasons: spring, summer, fall, and winter.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Find the four small card decks and shuffle each one separately. The green deck contains vine cards, the yellow deck has summer visitor cards, the purple deck has wine order cards, and the blue deck has winter visitor cards. Place each shuffled deck face down on its marked space on the board.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Shared Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the lira coins near the board where everyone can reach them. There are one-value bronze coins, two-value silver coins, and five-value gold coins. Also set out the grape and wine glass tokens nearby. These tokens track the values of grapes on your crush pad and wines in your cellar.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Vineyard Mats
walkthrough.text: Give each player a vineyard mat. This is your personal player board showing your three fields, crush pad, cellar, and structure spaces. Place your three field cards on the mat with the values five, six, and seven showing. These represent the three fields where you will plant vines.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching components: five regular worker meeples, one large grande worker meeple, a rooster wake-up token, a cork victory point marker, and a wine bottle residual payment marker. Place your cork on the starting space of the VP track and your wine bottle on the residual payment track.

walkthrough.title: Action Space Scaling
walkthrough.text: Let's adjust the board for our player count. With two players, only the leftmost action space is available for each action. With three or four players, the left and middle spaces are open. With five or six players, all three spaces are available. This keeps competition tight regardless of player count.

walkthrough.title: Choose a First Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly select a first player and give them the first player token. This token determines the order in which players place their roosters on the wake-up chart during spring. The first player token rotates counterclockwise at the end of each year.

walkthrough.title: Deal Mama and Papa Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal one mama card and one papa card face up to each player. The mama card gives you two regular workers and a specific set of starting cards drawn from the various decks. The papa card gives you your grande worker, usually some lira, and sometimes a choice between a structure and extra money.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Mama Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone resolves their mama card simultaneously. Each mama card specifies which cards you draw. For example, your mama might give you one vine card and one wine order card. Draw the specified cards from the appropriate decks. These are your starting hand.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Papa Cards
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player resolves their papa card. Take the lira shown on the card. If your papa offers a choice, such as a structure versus extra lira, make that choice now. Some papas are clearly better than others, but they all give you a workable start.

walkthrough.title: Optional Advanced Setup
walkthrough.text: For experienced players, there is an advanced setup variant. Instead of receiving one mama and one papa, deal two of each to every player and let them choose one mama and one papa to keep. This adds a strategic layer to the very first decision of the game.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Production Pipeline
walkthrough.text: Before we start, let's understand the core flow. You draw vine cards, plant them in fields, harvest grapes to your crush pad, make wine from grapes in your cellar, and fill wine orders for victory points. Every action in the game feeds into this pipeline. Setup is complete, so let's learn the seasons.

Every action feeds this pipeline

walkthrough.title: The Year Cycle
walkthrough.text: Viticulture is played over multiple years, each with five phases: spring, summer, fall, winter, and year end. Each season has specific actions. Spring is for choosing turn order. Summer and winter are for placing workers on action spaces. Fall is for drawing a visitor card. Year end is for cleanup and aging.

walkthrough.title: Spring: Choose Your Wake-Up Position
walkthrough.text: In spring, starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player places their rooster token on an open row of the wake-up chart numbered one through seven. Row one goes first but gets no bonus. Later rows go later but give better bonuses like free cards, lira, or victory points. The last row gives a temporary extra worker for the year.

walkthrough.title: Wake-Up Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what each wake-up row offers. Row one has no bonus. Row two gives a vine card. Row three gives a wine order card. Row four gives one lira or one victory point. Row five gives a summer or winter visitor card. Row six gives one victory point. Row seven gives the temporary worker for this year only.

walkthrough.title: Summer: Worker Placement
walkthrough.text: In summer, players take turns in wake-up order placing one worker at a time on summer action spaces. On your turn, place one worker on an available action space and immediately resolve that action. You can also choose to pass and save your remaining workers for winter. Once you pass, you cannot place more workers in summer.

walkthrough.title: Summer Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: The summer actions include drawing vine cards, planting vines in your fields, giving vineyard tours for lira, building structures on your vineyard mat, playing summer visitor cards for special abilities, and selling grapes or buying and selling fields. Each action space can only hold one worker unless you use your grande worker.

walkthrough.title: Planting Vines
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about planting. When you plant a vine, you place a vine card from your hand onto one of your three fields. Each vine card shows a value and may require a trellis, irrigation, or both as prerequisites. The total vine value in a field cannot exceed that field's number. So a seven-value field can hold vines totaling up to seven.

walkthrough.title: Building Structures
walkthrough.text: Structures are permanent improvements to your vineyard. The trellis and irrigation are prerequisites for planting certain vines. The yoke helps with harvesting. The windmill, cottage, and tasting room provide ongoing bonuses. The medium and large cellars let you store higher-value wines. Each structure can only be built once.

walkthrough.title: Fall: Draw a Visitor Card
walkthrough.text: In fall, each player in wake-up order draws one card. You choose to draw either a summer visitor card or a winter visitor card. Visitor cards are powerful one-time effects that can bend or break the normal rules. Choosing the right visitor card based on your current needs is a key skill.

walkthrough.title: Winter: Worker Placement
walkthrough.text: Winter works just like summer but with a different set of action spaces. Winter actions include harvesting grapes from your fields, making wine from grapes on your crush pad, filling wine orders for victory points, playing winter visitor cards, training new workers, and drawing wine order cards.

walkthrough.title: Harvesting Grapes
walkthrough.text: When you harvest, choose one of your fields that has planted vines. Take grape tokens equal to the vine values and place them on your crush pad. Red grapes go on the red side and white grapes on the white side. The value of each grape token matches the vine that produced it. Grapes age and increase in value during year end.

walkthrough.title: Making Wine
walkthrough.text: To make wine, you move grape tokens from your crush pad to your cellar. Red grapes make red wine, white grapes make white wine. You can also combine one red and one white grape to make blush wine, or combine two red and one white to make sparkling wine. The wine's value equals the sum of the grape values used.

walkthrough.title: Cellar Capacity
walkthrough.text: Your basic cellar can hold wines valued one through three. Building a medium cellar extends this to values four through six. Building a large cellar extends to values seven through nine. You cannot make wine whose value exceeds your cellar capacity. Planning your cellar upgrades is essential for filling high-value orders.

walkthrough.title: Filling Wine Orders
walkthrough.text: Wine order cards show the types and minimum values of wine required, plus the victory points awarded. To fill an order, you must have wines in your cellar that meet or exceed the requirements. The wines are discarded when you fill the order. Some orders also give residual payment bonuses.

walkthrough.title: The Grande Worker
walkthrough.text: Your grande worker is special. It can be placed on any action space even if all spaces for that action are already occupied. This is your anti-blocking tool. Save your grande worker for the one critical action that you absolutely cannot miss this season.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Action Spaces
walkthrough.text: Some action spaces have a bonus icon. If you place a worker on a bonus space, you get a small extra benefit like an additional card draw, extra lira, or a bonus victory point. However, the bonus space might get taken before you get there. The grande worker gets a bonus only if placed on the actual bonus space.

walkthrough.title: Training Workers
walkthrough.text: You start with only two or three regular workers plus your grande. To take more actions per year, you can train additional workers using a winter action. Training costs lira but is essential for competing in later years when you need to execute multiple steps of the pipeline in a single year.

walkthrough.title: Year End: Aging and Cleanup
walkthrough.text: At year end, several things happen in order. Grapes on your crush pad and wines in your cellar age, increasing each token's value by one up to the maximum your storage allows. Workers return to your supply. You collect residual payments. Roosters come off the wake-up chart. The first player token rotates counterclockwise.

walkthrough.title: Endgame and Victory
walkthrough.text: If any player reaches twenty or more victory points at any time, finish the current year and then the game ends. The player with the most victory points wins. Ties are broken by most lira, then highest total wine value in cellar, then highest total grape value on crush pad. You cannot go below negative five VP.

bullets: 5 phases: Spring → Summer → Fall → Winter → Year End
Repeats until someone reaches 20+ VP

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your Vineyard
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first year of Viticulture! We will walk through a practice game with four players. Each player has received their mama and papa cards and has their starting resources. The first-player token has been assigned. Let's begin with our first spring.

walkthrough.title: Spring: Choosing Wake-Up Order
walkthrough.text: The first player places their rooster on row one of the wake-up chart, choosing to go first with no bonus. The second player picks row three for a free wine order card. The third player takes row five for a visitor card. The fourth player takes row seven for the temporary worker, giving them four workers this year.

walkthrough.title: Summer Begins: Player One Plants
walkthrough.text: Player one goes first in summer. They have a vine card that requires no prerequisites, so they place a worker on the Plant action and plant it in their seven-value field. This vine produces one red grape worth three. Now they just need to harvest it later in winter.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Builds a Trellis
walkthrough.text: Player two has a valuable vine card but it requires a trellis. They place a worker on the Build action and pay the lira cost to build a trellis on their vineyard mat. Next year they will be able to plant that vine. Building infrastructure early is an investment that pays off later.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Gives a Tour
walkthrough.text: Player three needs money. They place a worker on the Give Tour action and gain two lira. This is a safe, reliable way to earn income but it does not advance the wine pipeline directly. Tours are a tempo play when you need lira for building or card costs.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Plays a Summer Visitor
walkthrough.text: Player four has a strong summer visitor card that lets them plant a vine and gain one lira. They place a worker on the Play Summer Visitor action and resolve the card. Visitor cards are powerful because they often let you do two things with one worker placement.

walkthrough.title: Continuing Summer and Passing
walkthrough.text: Players continue taking turns in wake-up order. Player one uses their second worker to draw vine cards for next year. Player two passes, saving their grande worker for winter. When you pass in a season, your remaining workers are still available for the next season.

walkthrough.title: Fall: Drawing Visitor Cards
walkthrough.text: In fall, each player draws one card. Player one picks a winter visitor, hoping for a powerful winter ability. Player two grabs a summer visitor for next year. Player three takes a winter visitor. Player four takes a summer visitor. This choice depends on what you need most right now.

walkthrough.title: Winter: Harvesting and Making Wine
walkthrough.text: Player one places a worker on the Harvest action and harvests their planted field. They take a red grape token worth three and place it on their crush pad. Then on their next turn, they place a worker on Make Wine and convert that grape into a red wine worth three in their cellar.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Forgetting Prerequisites
walkthrough.text: Player three tries to plant a vine that requires irrigation, but they have not built an irrigation structure yet. The plant action fails and the worker is wasted. Always check vine prerequisites before committing a worker to the plant action. This is one of the most common first-game mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Prerequisites Before Planting
walkthrough.text: Before placing a worker on the Plant action, verify that you have the required structures and available field capacity. Vines show trellis and irrigation icons next to their name. If you do not have the matching structure, you cannot plant that vine. Plan your builds one season ahead of your plants.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Uses the Grande Worker
walkthrough.text: Player two wants to harvest but the Harvest action space is already taken by Player one. Player two uses their grande worker to place on the occupied Harvest space anyway. The grande worker ignores blocking, making it the most valuable worker in the game. This is exactly the right time to use it.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Burning the Grande Early
walkthrough.text: Player four used their grande worker in summer on the Give Tour action just for extra lira, even though the space was not blocked. Now in winter, the Fill Order action is full and Player four cannot fill their wine order. Save the grande for moments when the action you need is blocked.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save the Grande for Blocked Actions
walkthrough.text: Your grande worker is your most precious resource each year. Never use it on an unblocked action space. Always save it for winter when competition for key actions like Harvest, Make Wine, and Fill Order gets fierce. The grande should be the last worker you place each year.

walkthrough.title: Year End: Aging and Cleanup
walkthrough.text: The first year ends. Grapes on crush pads and wines in cellars age, each increasing by one value. Player one's red wine three becomes a red wine four. All workers return home. Players collect any residual payments they have earned. Roosters come off the chart and the first-player token moves.

walkthrough.title: Year Two Preview
walkthrough.text: In year two, the players are more established. Player one fills their first wine order and scores four victory points. Player two plants their expensive vine and starts building toward blush wine. The pipeline is flowing now and the race to twenty points begins in earnest.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Imbalanced Seasons
walkthrough.text: Player three spent all their workers in summer building and planting, leaving none for winter. They could not harvest, make wine, or fill orders. The pipeline stalled completely. Always reserve workers for winter, where the high-VP scoring actions happen.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Summer and Winter Workers
walkthrough.text: Think of summer as investment and winter as payoff. Summer is for building structures and planting vines. Winter is for harvesting, making wine, and filling orders. If you spend all your workers in one season, you will bottleneck your pipeline. Plan a split before spring even starts.

walkthrough.title: The Race to Twenty
walkthrough.text: By year four, players are filling orders regularly. Player one reaches eighteen VP and needs one more big order. Player two is at fifteen but has high-value sparkling wine ready. The tension builds as everyone watches the VP track. Player one fills a final order and crosses twenty. The current year finishes and Player one wins with twenty-two points.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through your first harvest in Viticulture. You learned the seasonal rhythm, the production pipeline, the importance of prerequisites, when to save your grande worker, and how to balance summer and winter actions. Every game flows differently based on your cards and choices.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Grow Your Vineyard
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on wake-up selection, worker management, and pipeline optimization.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all actions, structures, and wine types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Build the Pipeline Early
walkthrough.text: In your first two years, focus on getting the vine-to-wine pipeline running. Draw vines, plant them, and set up your structures so that by year three you can harvest, make wine, and fill orders in a single winter. The sooner your pipeline is producing, the faster you score points.

walkthrough.title: Wake-Up Position Is Critical
walkthrough.text: Do not default to the same wake-up row every year. Choose based on what you need. If you must place on a contested action space this year, take an early row. If your plan is flexible and you need the bonus, take a later row. The temporary worker from row seven is especially powerful in busy years.

walkthrough.title: Respect Field Math
walkthrough.text: Each field has a capacity of five, six, or seven. Plan your plantings so that you maximize the value in each field without wasting capacity. A seven-value field should ideally hold vines totaling seven, not three. High-value plantings produce high-value grapes, which make high-value wines.

walkthrough.title: Save the Grande for Winter
walkthrough.text: Winter has the highest-value actions: harvest, make wine, and fill orders. These spaces fill up fast. Your grande worker should almost always be reserved for winter so you can guarantee access to the one critical action you need to score points this year.

walkthrough.title: Visitor Cards Are Game Changers
walkthrough.text: Visitor cards are extremely powerful because they let you do things that normally require a worker placement. Some visitors let you harvest and make wine in a single action. Others give free structures or VP bonuses. Always draw a visitor card in fall and look for cards that accelerate your pipeline.

walkthrough.title: Train Workers by Year Two
walkthrough.text: Starting with two or three workers is very tight. Training a fourth worker in year one or two dramatically increases your action count for the rest of the game. The lira investment pays for itself many times over. Do not wait until year four to expand your workforce.

walkthrough.title: Balance Income and Scoring
walkthrough.text: Lira is necessary for structures, training workers, and some card costs, but it does not win the game. Victory points win the game. Do not spend too many turns earning lira at the expense of advancing your wine production. Find the minimum income needed and invest the rest in scoring.

walkthrough.title: Plan for Blush and Sparkling Wine
walkthrough.text: Blush and sparkling wines use multiple grapes and produce higher-value results, which fill more lucrative orders. If you have both red and white vines planted, consider combining grapes into blush or sparkling wine. The medium and large cellar upgrades are essential for storing these premium wines.

summary.title: Build the Pipeline Early
bullets: Years 1-2: draw vines, plant, build structures
Year 3+: harvest, make wine, fill orders each winter

summary.title: Wake-Up Position Is Critical
bullets: Contested action needed → take early row
Flexible plan → take later row for bonus
Row 7 temp worker is powerful in busy years

summary.title: Respect Field Math
bullets: Maximize vine value in each field (5/6/7 capacity)
High-value plantings → high-value grapes → high-value wines

summary.title: Save the Grande for Winter
bullets: Winter actions (harvest, wine, fill) most contested
Grande guarantees access to critical scoring actions

summary.title: Visitor Cards Are Game Changers
bullets: Powerful effects that accelerate pipeline
Some combine 2 actions in 1 worker placement
Always draw a visitor in fall

summary.title: Train Workers by Year Two
bullets: 4th worker dramatically increases actions
Lira investment pays for itself quickly
Don't wait until year 4

summary.title: Balance Income and Scoring
bullets: Lira funds structures/training but doesn't win
VP wins → minimize income turns, maximize scoring

summary.title: Plan for Blush and Sparkling Wine
bullets: Combine red + white grapes → higher-value wines
Blush: 1R + 1W; Sparkling: 2R + 1W
Requires medium/large cellar upgrades

walkthrough.title: The Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: An aggressive strategy focuses on filling low-value orders as quickly as possible. Skip expensive structures and plant simple vines. Make cheap red or white wines and fill small orders for steady VP. This sacrifices late-game scoring potential for early momentum that can catch opponents off guard.

walkthrough.title: The Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach builds maximum infrastructure first. Construct trellis, irrigation, medium cellar, and possibly large cellar in years one and two. Then plant high-value vines and make premium sparkling wines worth seven or more. This starts slow but scores massive VP in the back half.

walkthrough.title: Visitor Card Combos
walkthrough.text: Some visitor card combinations can produce explosive turns. A winter visitor that lets you make wine combined with a fill-order action in the same winter can skip an entire season of setup. Learn the visitor deck contents and draft cards in fall that synergize with what you already have in hand.

walkthrough.title: Aging for Value
walkthrough.text: Grapes and wines age by one value each year. If you harvest a grape worth three and let it sit for two years, it becomes a five. This means you can intentionally delay making wine to let grapes age into higher-value results. Pair this with cellar upgrades to store the aged product.

walkthrough.title: Residual Payment Optimization
walkthrough.text: Some wine orders advance your residual payment marker. This gives you free lira every year at year end, which compounds over the game. Filling residual-granting orders early creates a passive income stream that funds structures and training without spending worker actions on tours.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Awareness
walkthrough.text: In three or four player games, action spaces fill up quickly. Watch which actions your opponents need and plan accordingly. If you know two opponents need to harvest this winter, you might take the Harvest action first to block them. Competitive blocking is a valid strategic tool.

walkthrough.title: The Cottage Rush
walkthrough.text: Building the cottage early gives you an extra visitor card draw every fall. Over the course of a six or seven year game, that is five or six extra visitor cards. Since visitor cards are the most powerful cards in the game, the cottage pays for itself many times over in strategic options.

walkthrough.title: Tempo Reading
walkthrough.text: Track all opponents' VP and estimate how many years remain. If someone is at fifteen VP and you are at eight, you cannot afford a slow engine build. Shift to short-term scoring plays. Conversely, if the game looks long, invest in infrastructure. Reading the game clock determines your entire strategy.

summary.title: The Rush Strategy
bullets: Fill low-value orders fast → skip expensive structures
Steady VP from cheap wines, catch opponents off guard

summary.title: The Engine Strategy
bullets: Build max infrastructure years 1-2
Premium sparkling wines → massive VP in back half

summary.title: Visitor Card Combos
bullets: Draft fall visitors that synergize with hand
Explosive turns from combined visitor + action effects

summary.title: Aging for Value
bullets: Grapes/wines +1 value per year end
Delay wine-making → grapes age into higher values
Requires cellar upgrades to store result

summary.title: Residual Payment Optimization
bullets: Fill residual-granting orders early → passive income
Compounds over game → funds structures without tours

summary.title: Blocking Awareness
bullets: Watch opponents' needs → take their actions first
Competitive blocking is valid in 3-4p games

summary.title: The Cottage Rush
bullets: Cottage → extra visitor card draw every fall
5-6 extra visitors over the game = massive options

summary.title: Tempo Reading
bullets: Track all VP → estimate years remaining
Behind? → short-term scoring. Ahead? → invest in engine
Game clock determines strategy

entries.definition: A green card representing a grape variety. Shows red and/or white grape values and may require trellis or irrigation to plant. Placed on fields.
A purple card showing required wine types and minimum values. Filling an order awards VP and sometimes advances the residual payment marker.
A yellow card with a one-time ability played during summer using a worker placement action. Effects range from free planting to bonus lira.
A blue card with a one-time ability played during winter using a worker placement action. Effects range from free wine-making to bonus VP.
A setup card dealt at the start of the game. Provides two regular workers and specifies which starting cards you draw from the various decks.
A setup card dealt at the start of the game. Provides your grande worker, starting lira, and sometimes a choice between a structure and extra money.
One of three areas on your vineyard mat where vines are planted. Fields have capacity values of five, six, or seven. Total vine value cannot exceed capacity.
The area on your vineyard mat where harvested grapes are stored. Divided into red and white sides. Grapes age here during year end.
The area on your vineyard mat where made wines are stored. Basic holds values one through three. Upgraded with medium and large cellar structures.
A standard meeple placed on action spaces. Each worker can be used once per year, either in summer or winter but not both.
A larger meeple that can be placed on any action space even if all spaces are occupied. Each player has exactly one grande worker.
A gray worker gained from wake-up row seven. Functions like a regular worker for one year only, then returns to the supply at year end.
Placed on the wake-up chart during spring to determine turn order and collect the row's bonus. Removed during year end.
The game's currency. Used to build structures, train workers, and pay certain card costs. Earned through tours, grape sales, visitor cards, and residual payments.
A grape token harvested from red vine values. Stored on the red side of the crush pad. Used to make red wine or combined for blush and sparkling.
A grape token harvested from white vine values. Stored on the white side of the crush pad. Used to make white wine or combined for blush and sparkling.
Made from one red grape. Value equals the grape's value. Stored in the cellar. Used to fill wine orders requiring red wine.
Made from one white grape. Value equals the grape's value. Stored in the cellar. Used to fill wine orders requiring white wine.
Made from one red grape plus one white grape. Value equals the sum of both grape values. Requires medium or large cellar for values above three.
Made from two red grapes plus one white grape. Value equals the sum of all three grape values. Typically requires large cellar due to high values.
A structure that must be built before planting vines that show a trellis icon. Costs lira to build. Placed on your vineyard mat.
A structure that must be built before planting vines that show an irrigation icon. Costs lira to build. Placed on your vineyard mat.
A structure that provides a harvesting bonus. Allows you to uproot planted vines (return them to hand) as an additional option.
A structure upgrade that extends cellar storage to wine values four through six. Required for storing most blush wines.
A structure upgrade that extends cellar storage to wine values seven through nine. Required for storing sparkling wines and aged premium wines.
A structure that grants an extra visitor card draw during fall. Provides compounding card advantage over the length of the game.
A structure that grants one VP each time you plant a vine. Powerful when combined with frequent planting turns.
A structure that grants one VP each time you give a tour. Turns the tour action into both income and scoring.
The first season of each year. Players place roosters on the wake-up chart to determine turn order and collect bonuses.
The second season. Players place workers on summer action spaces: draw vines, plant, build, tour, play summer visitors, sell/buy fields.
The third season. Each player draws one summer or winter visitor card of their choice.
The fourth season. Players place workers on winter action spaces: harvest, make wine, fill orders, play winter visitors, train workers, draw orders.
The cleanup phase. Grapes and wines age, workers return, residual payments collected, roosters removed, first-player token rotates counterclockwise.
During year end, each grape and wine token increases by one value, up to the maximum their storage allows. Key mechanic for producing premium wines.
Free lira collected at year end based on your position on the residual payment track. Advanced by filling certain wine orders. Compounds over the game.
The primary scoring currency. Earned mainly through filling wine orders and visitor card effects. First to twenty or more VP triggers endgame at year's end.
A specific action space that grants an extra small benefit when a worker is placed on it. Benefits include extra cards, lira, or VP.
A chart numbered one through seven where players place roosters in spring. Determines turn order for the entire year and grants row-specific bonuses.
